metadata.career_relevance: Every FAANG interview expects BOTH time AND space complexity. 'My solution is O(n) time and O(1) space' vs 'O(n) time but O(n) space' — the interviewer probes whether you understand the trade-off. Zerodha's embedded trading terminals have 512MB RAM — your O(n) extra space for a billion-trade dataset would crash the system.

hook.real_world_problem:
```
Zerodha's market data processor: 1 billion trade records per day
Dataset size: ~40GB in RAM

Algorithm with O(n) extra space:
  Creates a copy of 40GB = needs 80GB RAM
  AWS instance with 80GB: ₹15,000/hour
  In-place algorithm (O(1) extra space):
  Only needs ~40GB
  AWS instance with 64GB: ₹8,000/hour
  Savings: ₹7,000/hour = ₹1.68 crore/day

Space complexity directly impacts infrastructure costs.
Always state BOTH time AND space in interviews.
```
hook.what_you_will_build: Space analysis for common patterns — recursion stacks, memoization, in-place algorithms.

concept_explanation.space_definitions:
**Two types of space:**

```
TOTAL SPACE = Input Space + Auxiliary Space

Input Space: memory used to store inputs (usually not counted)
Auxiliary Space: EXTRA memory your algorithm uses

Big-O space complexity = Auxiliary Space

O(1) space — constant: fixed variables, no scaling with input
  int x = 5; boolean flag = true; (not arrays)

O(log n) space — recursion on halved problem
  Binary search (recursive): log n stack frames

O(n) space — storing n elements
  Copy of input array, HashMap with n entries, memoization table

O(n²) space — n × n structure
  2D matrix, adjacency matrix for n nodes
```

visual_aid.description: Two diagrams side by side. Left: Recursion call stack for binarySearch(n=16) — 4 stack frames (log₂16=4), each frame holds lo, hi, mid, target. Labeled 'O(log n) space'. Right: Fibonacci recursion — deep left branch fib(n)→fib(n-1)→...→fib(0), depth n frames. Labeled 'O(n) space'. Bottom: comparison table: Algorithm | Time | Space. Iterative binary search: O(log n) | O(1). Recursive binary search: O(log n) | O(log n). Merge sort: O(n log n) | O(n). In-place sort (heapsort): O(n log n) | O(1).
visual_aid.alt_text: Recursion stack depth determines space complexity

worked_example.scenario: Compare space complexity of 3 sorting algorithms.
worked_example.problem_statement: State time AND space complexity for: (1) merge sort, (2) quicksort, (3) heapsort.

step_by_step_solution.details: Time: O(n log n). Space: O(n) — creates temporary arrays in merge step. Plus O(log n) recursion stack. Total: O(n).

worked_example.expected_output:
If space is premium: heapsort (O(1) space)
If stability needed: merge sort (stable, O(n) space)
Default Java: TimSort (merge sort variant, O(n) space)
things_to_notice: Space complexity = auxiliary space, NOT input space. You don't count the n-element input array itself.
Recursion has implicit space cost: each function call adds a frame to the call stack. Deep recursion can cause StackOverflowError for large n.
Time-space trade-off: memoization converts O(2ⁿ) time → O(n) time, but costs O(n) space. In-place algorithms save space but may be slower or harder to implement.

industry_examples.use_case: 512MB RAM limit. Sorting 10M trades: merge sort needs 40MB extra, heapsort needs ~0 extra. For embedded systems, O(1) space algorithms are mandatory.

applications.interview_relevance: Always say both: 'O(n log n) time and O(1) space'. Never just time. Interviewers specifically ask 'Can you do it with O(1) extra space?' — usually implies in-place algorithm.
applications.real_world_workflow: Space analysis: (1) identify all data structures created (arrays, lists, maps), (2) determine their sizes in terms of n, (3) for recursion: determine max call depth, (4) total = dominant term, (5) state with justification.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Saying binary search is O(1) space because 'it doesn't create any arrays'. Recursive binary search creates O(log n) stack frames.
common_gaps.remediation: For any recursive function: space = O(max recursion depth). Binary search: depth = log n. Naive recursion: depth = n. Iterative version of binary search: O(1) space (no recursion stack).
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Student says recursive binary search is O(1) space

key_takeaways: Space complexity = auxiliary space only (not counting input)
Recursion: each call frame = stack space, max depth = space cost
Iterative vs recursive: same time, different space (iterative = O(1) for simple cases)
Memoization trade-off: O(2ⁿ) time → O(n) time, costs O(n) space
In-place algorithm: O(1) extra space (modifies input directly)
Merge sort: O(n log n) time, O(n) space. Heapsort: O(n log n) time, O(1) space
Always state BOTH in interviews: 'O(x) time and O(y) space'

self_check_questions.question: Why does recursive Fibonacci use O(n) space even though it makes O(2ⁿ) calls?
self_check_questions.answer: The 2ⁿ calls don't all exist simultaneously — they're sequential. The maximum number of stack frames at any one time is the depth of the deepest path in the recursion tree. For Fibonacci, the left branch goes fib(n) → fib(n-1) → fib(n-2) → ... → fib(0), which is n frames deep. That's O(n) space. The 2ⁿ time complexity is about total work done, not simultaneous work.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: Best/Worst/Average Case Analysis — the same algorithm can have wildly different performance depending on input.
